summary:
The Norman Kingdom of Sicily (1130–1194 AD) was one of the most remarkable multicultural states of medieval Europe, established by Roger II (the first king) from the Norman adventurers who had carved out territories in southern Italy and Sicily during the 11th century, and distinguished by a sophisticated synthesis of Latin Christian, Arab Muslim, and Greek Byzantine cultures under Norman military domination. Sicily had been under Arab rule from 827 to 1072 and under Byzantine rule before that; its Norman conquerors inherited and presided over a uniquely multicultural population and administrative tradition.

Roger II's court at Palermo was one of the intellectual wonders of the 12th century: Arabic was an official language alongside Latin and Greek; Arab scholars served at court alongside Norman knights; Islamic geometric decoration adorned Norman-built churches (the Cappella Palatina in Palermo fused Byzantine mosaics, Arabic muqarnas ceiling, and Norman arches in a single astonishing space); and the Arab geographer al-Idrisi compiled the most accurate medieval world map under Roger's patronage — the 'Tabula Rogeriana' (1154), the most complete and accurate world map produced anywhere until the 15th century. The kingdom's administrative system incorporated Byzantine and Arab fiscal and legal techniques alongside Norman feudalism, creating a governmental synthesis of unusual sophistication. The Norman Kingdom passed to the Hohenstaufen dynasty through marriage, and its multicultural achievement was continued under [[event:3042|Emperor Frederick II]], before the island's special character was gradually eroded by subsequent conquests.
